viral_illness.name: Viral Illness

doorNote.nurseNotes: Mom reports child has had fever for 2 days, runny nose, and cough. Drinking less but still having wet diapers. No rash. Several kids at daycare are sick. Immunizations up to date.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about the fever - how high has it been and does it come down with Tylenol?
historyQuestions.answer: It's been up to 103 at night. Tylenol brings it down to about 100 and he feels better, but it comes back in 4-5 hours.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Fever responsive to antipyretics is reassuring; typical viral pattern

historyQuestions.text: What other symptoms has he had?
historyQuestions.answer: Runny nose with clear mucus, cough that's worse at night, and he says his throat hurts a little.
historyQuestions.reasoning: URI symptoms with fever typical of viral illness

historyQuestions.text: How is his activity level? Is he playing at all?
historyQuestions.answer: When the fever is down he'll play with his toys and watch TV. When it's high he just wants to lay on the couch.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Return to baseline activity with fever reduction is very reassuring

historyQuestions.text: How much is he drinking and eating?
historyQuestions.answer: He's not eating much - maybe half of what he usually eats. But he's drinking juice and water, probably 4-5 cups a day.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Maintained oral intake critical for outpatient management

historyQuestions.text: How many wet diapers has he had?
historyQuestions.answer: He's still in pull-ups at night. He had 4-5 wet diapers yesterday. They seemed normal amount.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Normal urine output indicates adequate hydration

historyQuestions.text: Have you noticed any rash?
historyQuestions.answer: No rash anywhere.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Absence of petechiae/purpura reassuring against meningococcemia

historyQuestions.text: Is he moving his neck normally? Any stiffness?
historyQuestions.answer: He's moving it fine. He looked around when the dog barked.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Normal neck mobility argues against meningitis

historyQuestions.text: Has he been around anyone sick?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, there are like 5 kids out sick from his daycare room this week.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Sick contacts strongly suggest viral transmission

historyQuestions.text: Are his shots up to date?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, he's had all his vaccines on schedule.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Fully immunized status reduces risk of serious bacterial infection

historyQuestions.text: Does he have any medical problems?
historyQuestions.answer: No, he's been healthy. This is the first time he's really been sick.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No underlying conditions reduces risk

historyQuestions.text: Has he been pulling at his ears?
historyQuestions.answer: Maybe a little, now that you mention it. He was rubbing his right ear yesterday.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Ear tugging may indicate otitis media as complication of URI

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you currently taking?
historyQuestions.answer: Just blood pressure medicine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medications can cause symptoms and affect treatment choices.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No known drug allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical for safe prescribing.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

physicalExam.finding: HR 130 (appropriate for fever), RR 28, Temp 102.4°F. SpO2 98%.
physicalExam.reasoning: Vital signs appropriate for degree of fever; no respiratory distress

physicalExam.finding: Alert, interactive toddler. Makes eye contact. Reaches for mom when approached. Smiles. Not toxic-appearing.
physicalExam.reasoning: Well-appearing, interactive child = low risk for serious bacterial infection

physicalExam.finding: Ears: Right TM erythematous, mildly bulging with decreased mobility. Left TM clear. Eyes: No conjunctival injection. Nose: Clear rhinorrhea. Throat: Mild pharyngeal erythema, no exudate, tonsils 2+ without exudate.
physicalExam.reasoning: Found right otitis media as complication of viral URI

physicalExam.finding: Supple, no meningismus. Small anterior cervical lymphadenopathy bilaterally.
physicalExam.reasoning: No meningismus reassuring; reactive lymphadenopathy expected with URI

physicalExam.finding: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, crackles, or stridor. No increased work of breathing.
physicalExam.reasoning: Clear lungs rules out lower respiratory infection

physicalExam.finding: Tachycardic but regular. No murmur. Capillary refill <2 seconds.
physicalExam.reasoning: Good perfusion indicates adequate hydration

physicalExam.finding: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly.
physicalExam.reasoning: Rules out abdominal source

physicalExam.finding: No rash, petechiae, or purpura. Warm, pink, well-perfused.
physicalExam.reasoning: Absence of petechiae/purpura reassuring

Viral Syndrome Only
keyFeatures: Would explain all symptoms even without ear finding

differentials.name: Serious Bacterial Infection
keyFeatures: Well-appearing, immunized, no focal concern beyond ear

viral_illness.correctDiagnosis: Viral URI with Acute Otitis Media
viral_illness.teachingPoint: Febrile children evaluation focuses on: 1) Toxic vs non-toxic appearance (this is the single most important factor), 2) Age (under 3 months = automatic high risk), 3) Immunization status, 4) Presence of focal infection source, 5) Hydration status. This well-appearing, immunized toddler with URI symptoms and sick contacts can be managed as outpatient. AOM treatment: Amoxicillin 80-90mg/kg/day OR observation for 48-72 hours if over age 2 with mild symptoms.
viral_illness.clinicalPearl: The 'well-appearing febrile child' has a very low risk of serious bacterial infection if they are interactive, consolable, and have normal mental status. Trust your gut - a truly sick child looks sick. The ear infection is almost incidental here - many would observe rather than treat given age and mild symptoms.

serious_infection.name: Serious Bacterial Infection

doorNote.vitals: HR 180, RR 48, Temp 104.2°F (40.1°C), SpO2 94% on RA, Weight 8kg
doorNote.chiefComplaint: High fever and not acting right
doorNote.nurseNotes: Mom reports baby has had fever since last night, not eating, very fussy. Had one episode of vomiting. Not as active as usual. Parents appear very worried. Up to date on vaccines for age.

historyQuestions.text: When did the fever start and how high has it been?
historyQuestions.answer: It started last night around midnight. It was 104 when I checked. I gave Tylenol but it only went down to 102 and she was still really fussy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: High fever with poor response to antipyretics concerning

historyQuestions.text: You mentioned she's not acting right - what do you mean?
historyQuestions.answer: She's usually so happy and smiley. Now she just lies there and whimpers. She won't play with her toys. She barely looks at me.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Irritability, lethargy, and poor interaction are red flags

historyQuestions.text: How has her feeding been?
historyQuestions.answer: She won't breastfeed at all today. I tried a bottle and she took maybe 2 ounces and then vomited. She hasn't had anything in 6 hours.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Poor feeding and vomiting concerning for significant illness

historyQuestions.text: How many wet diapers has she had?
historyQuestions.answer: Only 2 wet diapers since last night. Usually she has way more than that.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Decreased urine output indicates dehydration

historyQuestions.text: Have you noticed any rash or spots?
historyQuestions.answer: I noticed some tiny red dots on her tummy and legs this morning. They don't go away when I press on them.
historyQuestions.reasoning: PETECHIAE - this is an emergency finding

historyQuestions.text: Have you noticed anything about the soft spot on her head?
historyQuestions.answer: Actually it does look like it's sticking up more than usual. Is that bad?
historyQuestions.reasoning: Bulging fontanelle suggests increased intracranial pressure

historyQuestions.text: How is her breathing?
historyQuestions.answer: It seems faster than normal. She's kind of grunting sometimes.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Tachypnea and grunting are signs of respiratory distress or metabolic acidosis

historyQuestions.text: Has she been around anyone sick?
historyQuestions.answer: Her 4-year-old brother had a cold last week but he's better now. No one else is sick.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Sick contacts possible but presentation concerning for more than viral illness

historyQuestions.text: Are her vaccines up to date?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, she had her 6-month shots about 2 months ago.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Vaccines reduce but don't eliminate risk of invasive bacterial disease

historyQuestions.text: Was she born healthy? Any medical problems?
historyQuestions.answer: She was born at 35 weeks but was fine, didn't need NICU. No problems since then.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Premature birth may confer some increased risk

physicalExam.finding: HR 180 (very tachycardic), RR 48 with grunting, Temp 104.2°F, SpO2 94% on RA. Poor perfusion noted - CRT 4 seconds.
physicalExam.reasoning: ABNORMAL vital signs with poor perfusion = septic shock until proven otherwise

physicalExam.finding: Ill-appearing, lethargic infant. Difficult to console. Weak cry. Poor eye contact. Does not track visually. Mottled skin.
physicalExam.reasoning: TOXIC APPEARANCE - this child is critically ill

physicalExam.finding: Anterior fontanelle bulging and tense.
physicalExam.reasoning: Bulging fontanelle in febrile infant = meningitis until proven otherwise

physicalExam.finding: Petechial rash on trunk and lower extremities. Some lesions are coalescing into purpura. Skin mottled with delayed capillary refill.
physicalExam.reasoning: PETECHIAE/PURPURA with fever = meningococcemia - LIFE-THREATENING EMERGENCY

physicalExam.finding: Difficult to assess meningismus in infant but resists passive flexion.
physicalExam.reasoning: Nuchal rigidity supports meningitis diagnosis

physicalExam.finding: Tachycardic, thready pulses. Cool extremities.
physicalExam.reasoning: Signs of poor perfusion and shock

physicalExam.finding: Tachypneic with subcostal retractions and grunting. Lungs clear to auscultation.
physicalExam.reasoning: Respiratory distress despite clear lungs suggests compensatory tachypnea for acidosis

physicalExam.finding: Soft but mildly distended. Decreased bowel sounds.
physicalExam.reasoning: Ileus can occur with sepsis

differentials.name: Bacterial Meningitis (other pathogen)
keyFeatures: Could be pneumococcal or H. flu despite vaccines

differentials.name: Septic Shock (other source)
keyFeatures: Could be UTI or pneumonia gone septic

differentials.name: Viral Illness
keyFeatures: Too sick, petechiae not explained by virus

serious_infection.teachingPoint: This is a PEDIATRIC EMERGENCY. The triad of fever + petechiae + ill appearance = meningococcemia until proven otherwise. IMMEDIATE ACTIONS: 1) ABCs - this child may need intubation, 2) IV/IO access IMMEDIATELY, 3) Fluid bolus 20mL/kg NS - may need repeat, 4) Empiric antibiotics NOW (ceftriaxone 100mg/kg IV) - do NOT wait for LP, 5) Blood cultures if possible but don't delay antibiotics, 6) Prepare for LP when stabilized, 7) Dexamethasone if meningitis suspected, 8) Vasopressors if fluid-refractory shock. Contact public health for prophylaxis of close contacts.
serious_infection.clinicalPearl: NEVER IGNORE PETECHIAE IN A FEBRILE CHILD. This is one of the few true emergencies where minutes matter. Give antibiotics immediately - do not wait for cultures, do not wait for LP, do not wait for anything. A bulging fontanelle in a febrile infant is meningitis until proven otherwise. Trust the parents when they say 'something is wrong' - they know their child.

female-2-fever.name: Linda Rodriguez
female-2-fever.variantDescription: Febrile toddler - otitis media vs more serious infection

missedConsequences.Acute Otitis Media: Usually self-limited but untreated can lead to complications (mastoiditis, meningitis).
missedConsequences.Meningitis: Bacterial meningitis has high mortality and morbidity if missed.
missedConsequences.UTI: Missed UTI can cause pyelonephritis, renal scarring, sepsis.
missedConsequences.Bacteremia: Occult bacteremia can progress to sepsis.

commonPitfalls: Not recognizing toxic vs non-toxic appearance
Missing UTI - always consider in febrile young children without clear source
Over-relying on normal vitals (kids compensate until they crash)
Missing subtle signs of meningitis in young children
Not doing complete exam including ear, throat, skin

mcqs.question: In a febrile 2-year-old without obvious source, what is the MOST common serious bacterial infection?

options.explanation: Serious but less common than UTI.
UTI is the most common serious bacterial infection in young febrile children. Must always consider, especially in girls and uncircumcised boys.
Would typically have respiratory symptoms or findings.
Much less common since pneumococcal vaccine.

mcqs.question: Which finding would be MOST concerning for serious bacterial infection in this child?

options.explanation: Height of fever alone doesn't distinguish serious vs benign infection.

options.text: Ill-appearing, not making eye contact, inconsolable
options.explanation: Clinical appearance is the best predictor of serious illness. A 'toxic-appearing' child warrants aggressive workup.
Suggests viral URI, actually makes serious bacterial infection less likely.
Expected with fever - HR increases ~10 bpm per degree Celsius of fever.

female-2-fever.oldcartsNarrative: [Mother speaking]: "She's had a fever for 2 days now. It was 103°F at home. She's been fussy and pulling at her right ear. Not eating as well but drinking okay. Maybe a little runny nose. No cough. No vomiting or diarrhea. She peed normally in her diaper. She got her shots at her 2-year checkup a month ago. She was at daycare earlier this week. No sick contacts that I know of. She's never been this sick before."

historyQuestions.text: How long has she had the fever?
historyQuestions.answer: Started 2 days ago. It's been going up and down but always coming back high.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Duration helps determine if viral (usually peaks day 2-3) or bacterial.

historyQuestions.text: How high has the fever been?
historyQuestions.answer: 103°F at home. Maybe higher - I gave her Tylenol.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Temperature height alone doesn't distinguish serious vs benign, but very high fever (>104°F) warrants careful evaluation.

historyQuestions.text: How is she acting? Is she playful or lethargic?
historyQuestions.answer: She's cranky and fussy, but she does perk up and play a little after Tylenol. She smiled at me this morning.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CRITICAL: Toxic appearance (lethargic, not making eye contact, inconsolable) suggests serious infection. This child sounds non-toxic.

historyQuestions.text: You mentioned she's pulling at her ear?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, the right one mainly. She keeps rubbing it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Ear tugging is classic for AOM in toddlers who can't verbalize pain.

historyQuestions.text: How is she eating and drinking?
historyQuestions.answer: Not eating much solid food, but she's drinking juice and milk pretty well.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Adequate fluid intake is reassuring. Poor intake suggests more serious illness or dehydration.

historyQuestions.text: Is she urinating normally? How many wet diapers?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, I think so. At least 4-5 wet diapers today.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Normal urine output reassuring for hydration. Also relevant for UTI consideration.

historyQuestions.text: Any runny nose, cough, or congestion?
historyQuestions.answer: A little runny nose the last few days. No real cough.
historyQuestions.reasoning: URI symptoms often precede AOM. If clear source, less likely occult infection.

historyQuestions.text: Any rash?
historyQuestions.answer: No rash that I've seen.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Petechial/purpuric rash would be concerning for meningococcemia.

historyQuestions.text: Has she been moving her neck normally?
historyQuestions.answer: I think so. She looks around when I call her name.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Meningismus assessment - though young children may not show classic signs.

historyQuestions.text: Is she up to date on her vaccinations?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, she got all her shots at her 2-year checkup last month.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Fully vaccinated = lower risk of invasive pneumococcal and H. flu disease.

historyQuestions.text: Does she go to daycare?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, 3 days a week. She was there Monday and Tuesday.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Daycare is major risk factor for AOM and viral infections.

historyQuestions.text: Any medical problems or prior ear infections?
historyQuestions.answer: No, she's been healthy. This is her first real illness.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prior ear infections would increase AOM probability.

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you currently taking?
historyQuestions.answer: Just blood pressure medicine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medications can cause symptoms and affect treatment choices.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No known drug allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical for safe prescribing.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

physicalExam.finding: Temp 103.5°F (39.7°C), HR 140, RR 28, SpO2 98%. HR elevated but appropriate for fever.
physicalExam.reasoning: Tachycardia expected with fever. Assess if disproportionate to fever.

physicalExam.finding: Fussy but consolable toddler. Makes eye contact with mother. Cries with exam but calms when held. Not toxic-appearing.
physicalExam.reasoning: CRITICAL: Non-toxic appearance is very reassuring. This is the most important assessment.

physicalExam.text: Head, eyes, ears, nose, throat exam
physicalExam.finding: Anterior fontanelle closed (normal for age). TMs: Right - erythematous, bulging, purulent fluid behind drum, decreased mobility, no light reflex. Left - normal. Pharynx mildly erythematous. Clear rhinorrhea.
physicalExam.reasoning: DIAGNOSTIC: Bulging TM with effusion and erythema is diagnostic for AOM.

physicalExam.finding: Supple. No meningismus. No lymphadenopathy. Moves neck freely.
physicalExam.reasoning: No meningeal signs - reassuring against meningitis.

physicalExam.finding: Clear breath sounds bilaterally. No retractions. No tachypnea. Good air entry.
physicalExam.reasoning: Normal chest exam makes pneumonia unlikely.

physicalExam.finding: Tachycardic but regular rhythm. No murmur. Good perfusion, cap refill <2 sec.
physicalExam.reasoning: Good perfusion is reassuring.

physicalExam.finding: Soft, non-tender. No masses. Normal bowel sounds.
physicalExam.reasoning: Benign abdominal exam.

physicalExam.finding: No rash. No petechiae. Warm, well-perfused.
physicalExam.reasoning: No petechial rash - reassuring against meningococcemia.

labs.name: Labs not required for typical AOM in non-toxic child
labs.result: With clear source (AOM) identified in well-appearing, fully vaccinated toddler, extensive laboratory workup is not indicated.
labs.reasoning: A non-toxic appearing, fully vaccinated child with identified source (AOM) does not need routine labs. Clinical appearance is the best predictor of serious illness.

labs.name: Urinalysis (catheterized specimen in non-toilet trained)
labs.result: Would obtain if no clear source found on exam. In this case: Clear source identified - UA deferred unless clinical concern.
labs.reasoning: UTI is the most common serious bacterial infection in young febrile children. ALWAYS consider if no clear source identified. Bag specimens are unreliable - need cath or clean catch.

labs.name: CBC with differential
labs.result: Not routinely needed with clear source in well-appearing child
labs.reasoning: In pre-vaccine era, WBC helped predict occult bacteremia. Post-vaccine (PCV13, Hib), risk of occult bacteremia in well-appearing, vaccinated children is very low. CBC not routinely indicated.

labs.name: Blood culture
labs.result: Not indicated for typical AOM in well-appearing child
labs.reasoning: Blood culture indicated for: toxic-appearing children, ill-appearing with no source, concern for sepsis. Not for straightforward AOM.

labs.name: Procalcitonin
labs.reasoning: Procalcitonin may help distinguish bacterial vs viral in unclear cases. Not needed when source is identified.

imaging.name: No imaging indicated
imaging.result: Imaging not required for typical AOM with benign examination
imaging.reasoning: AOM is a clinical diagnosis. No imaging needed. Would only consider imaging if: suspected mastoiditis (post-auricular swelling/tenderness), meningitis, or complicated course.

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.result: Not indicated - no respiratory symptoms or abnormal chest exam
imaging.reasoning: CXR indicated for: tachypnea, hypoxia, respiratory distress, abnormal lung exam, or high fever without source. Not needed with identified source and clear lungs.

procedures.name: Pneumatic otoscopy
procedures.result: Decreased mobility of right TM confirming middle ear effusion. Left TM mobile.
procedures.reasoning: Gold standard for assessing TM mobility and middle ear effusion. Immobile or hypomobile TM confirms effusion. Essential skill for AOM diagnosis.

procedures.name: Lumbar puncture
procedures.result: NOT indicated - child is non-toxic appearing with no meningeal signs and clear alternative diagnosis
procedures.reasoning: LP indicated for: toxic-appearing child, meningeal signs, concern for meningitis. NOT indicated in well-appearing child with identified source (AOM).

treatments.name: Amoxicillin (high-dose)
treatments.result: Amoxicillin 80-90 mg/kg/day divided BID x 10 days. For a 12kg child: ~500mg BID (using liquid formulation).
treatments.reasoning: FIRST-LINE for AOM per AAP guidelines. High-dose amoxicillin overcomes resistant S. pneumoniae. Duration: 10 days for <2 years, 5-7 days for ≥2 years with non-severe.

treatments.name: Amoxicillin-clavulanate (Augmentin)
treatments.result: Second-line for: treatment failure after 48-72 hours, recent amoxicillin use (<30 days), concurrent purulent conjunctivitis (suggests H. flu).
treatments.reasoning: Add clavulanate if concern for beta-lactamase producing H. flu or M. cat. Also first-line if amoxicillin failure. 90 mg/kg/day amoxicillin component.

treatments.name: Observation option (watchful waiting)
treatments.result: Per AAP: Consider observation for 48-72 hours for unilateral, non-severe AOM in children ≥6 months with access to follow-up.
treatments.reasoning: AAP allows observation for: ≥6 months old, non-severe (mild otalgia <48h, temp <102.2°F), unilateral. Must have assured follow-up. This child is 2 years with clear AOM - antibiotics reasonable.

treatments.name: Ibuprofen for fever and pain
treatments.result: Ibuprofen 10 mg/kg every 6 hours PRN for fever and pain. More effective than acetaminophen for ear pain.
treatments.reasoning: Pain management is crucial in AOM. Ibuprofen preferred for anti-inflammatory effect. Can alternate with acetaminophen if needed.

treatments.name: Acetaminophen (Tylenol)
treatments.result: Acetaminophen 15 mg/kg every 4-6 hours PRN, max 5 doses/day
treatments.reasoning: Alternative or adjunct to ibuprofen for fever/pain. Can alternate with ibuprofen for persistent fever.

treatments.name: Benzocaine otic drops
treatments.result: NOT recommended - risk of methemoglobinemia in young children
treatments.reasoning: AAP recommends AGAINST benzocaine in children <2 due to risk of methemoglobinemia. Use systemic analgesics instead.

treatments.name: Decongestants/antihistamines
treatments.result: NOT effective for AOM and not recommended in young children
treatments.reasoning: No evidence of benefit for AOM. Antihistamines/decongestants not recommended in children <4 due to lack of efficacy and safety concerns.

consults.name: No specialty consultation needed for typical AOM
consults.result: Primary care can manage uncomplicated AOM
consults.reasoning: Straightforward AOM is managed in primary care. Referral not needed.

consults.name: ENT referral
consults.result: Consider for: recurrent AOM (≥3 in 6 months or ≥4 in 1 year), chronic OME >3 months, suspected cholesteatoma, mastoiditis.
consults.reasoning: ENT referral for: recurrent AOM (tympanostomy tube consideration), chronic effusion with hearing concerns, complications.

disposition.name: Discharge home with oral antibiotics and follow-up
disposition.result: Discharge with amoxicillin and ibuprofen. Return precautions given: worsening symptoms, not improving in 48-72 hours, new symptoms (rash, vomiting). Follow up in 10-14 days or sooner if not improving.
disposition.reasoning: AOM is outpatient diagnosis. Clear return precautions essential. Follow up to ensure resolution.

disposition.name: Hospital admission
disposition.result: NOT indicated for uncomplicated AOM
disposition.reasoning: Admit only for: toxic appearance, suspected mastoiditis or meningitis, unable to tolerate PO, severe dehydration, concern for serious bacterial infection.

actualDiagnosis.name: Acute Otitis Media, Right Ear
teachingPoints: Clinical appearance (toxic vs non-toxic) is the most important assessment in pediatric fever
AOM diagnosis requires: Middle ear effusion AND signs of middle ear inflammation (bulging, erythema, otalgia)
AAP guidelines for AOM treatment: <6 months - always treat; 6mo-2yr - treat if bilateral or severe; >2yr - can observe for 48-72hr if unilateral and non-severe
First-line antibiotic: Amoxicillin 80-90 mg/kg/day divided BID x 10 days
Always consider UTI in young febrile children without clear source
Post-vaccine era: occult bacteremia much less common in well-appearing, vaccinated children

revisedDifferential.name: UTI
keyFeatures: Would check UA if no source found

female-2-fever.teachingPoint: This is classic AOM in a toddler. On your pediatrics rotation, you'll see this daily. Key points: 1) Always assess 'toxic vs non-toxic' first - this drives everything, 2) Examine both ears - often bilateral, 3) Know the AAP treatment guidelines - not everyone needs antibiotics, 4) If no source found, think UTI - it's the most common serious bacterial infection in young children, 5) A fully vaccinated child who looks well has low risk of bacteremia - trust your clinical assessment.
female-2-fever.clinicalPearl: The Yale Observation Scale assesses: quality of cry, reaction to parents, state variation, color, hydration, and response to social overtures. A well-appearing child who makes eye contact, is consolable, and interacts with caregivers is low risk. The adage 'trust the mother' applies - if mom says 'something is really wrong,' believe her even if exam is reassuring.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient was a 3-week-old with fever?
whatIfScenarios.description: Fever in newborn (<28 days) - different approach needed

chiefComplaint.modified: 3-week-old with temperature 100.8°F rectally. Mom says 'just not acting right.'

expectedReasoning.topDiagnosis: Serious bacterial infection until proven otherwise

feedback.correct: NEONATAL FEVER IS AN EMERGENCY. Any fever (≥100.4°F/38°C) in <28 days = full sepsis workup: CBC, blood culture, UA + culture, LP + CSF studies. Start empiric antibiotics (ampicillin + gentamicin or cefotaxime). Consider HSV (add acyclovir if any risk factors). ADMIT all febrile neonates.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of high-risk age. What's the full workup?
feedback.incorrect: Febrile neonate (<28 days): 10-15% have serious bacterial infection. MANDATORY workup: blood culture, urine (cath or suprapubic), LP, CBC. Empiric antibiotics: ampicillin + gentamicin (or cefotaxime). Add acyclovir if HSV risk (maternal history, vesicles, seizures, LFT abnormalities). Hospital admission for all.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: 18-month-old with fever = can often observe if well-appearing. <28 days with fever = full sepsis workup and admit regardless of appearance. Age completely changes approach.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the child had fever and petechial rash?
whatIfScenarios.description: Fever with petechiae - concern for meningococcemia

chiefComplaint.modified: Fever 103°F, looks ill, mom noticed purple dots on legs that don't blanch when pressed

feedback.correct: FEVER + PETECHIAE + ILL = MENINGOCOCCEMIA UNTIL PROVEN OTHERWISE. Don't wait for LP - give ceftriaxone IMMEDIATELY. This progresses in hours. Blood cultures, CBC, coags. ICU. Notify infection control. Meningococcal disease can kill within 12 hours. Petechiae above nipple line = higher risk.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of meningococcemia. What's the time pressure?
feedback.incorrect: Meningococcemia: fever + petechiae + ill = emergency. Give ceftriaxone NOW - don't delay for LP if unstable. Petechiae that spread rapidly are ominous. Watch for purpura fulminans (DIC). ICU admission. Close contacts need prophylaxis (rifampin or ciprofloxacin). This kills in hours.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Fever alone in well-appearing child = often observation. Fever + petechiae + ill = immediate antibiotics, ICU. Rash changes everything.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if no source was found after complete exam?

chiefComplaint.modified: Same fever but ears are clear, lungs clear, no rash, no meningismus, well-appearing. Vaccinations up to date.

expectedReasoning.topDiagnosis: Fever without source - UTI most likely SBI
keyDiscriminators: UTI is most common SBI in this age

feedback.correct: Fever without source in well-appearing vaccinated toddler: UTI is the most common serious bacterial infection. Get urine (cath or clean catch) - UA AND culture. If well-appearing and vaccinated, risk of occult bacteremia is <1%. Can often observe with close follow-up rather than empiric antibiotics.
feedback.partial: Good thinking about UTI. What about bacteremia risk?
feedback.incorrect: Fever without source: UTI is most common SBI (7-8% of febrile young children without source). Always check urine in children <2 with fever without source. Post-vaccine era: occult bacteremia risk very low (<1%) in well-appearing, vaccinated children. Close follow-up within 24 hours if not treating empirically.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Clear source (like AOM) = treat the source. No source = UTI most likely SBI, check urine. Well-appearing vaccinated child has low bacteremia risk - can observe with follow-up.

guidelineReferences.name: AAP Acute Otitis Media Guidelines 2013
keyPoints: Diagnosis requires middle ear effusion PLUS acute inflammation
Amoxicillin 80-90 mg/kg/day first-line if antibiotics indicated
Observation option for mild AOM in children ≥6 months
Watchful waiting: adequate follow-up and pain management
Augmentin if treatment failure or recent antibiotics
Avoid decongestants and antihistamines - not effective

toddler-18mo-aom.name: Lily P.
toddler-18mo-aom.chiefComplaint: Pulling at her ear and crying
toddler-18mo-aom.variantDescription: Classic acute otitis media in a toddler

missedConsequences.Acute Otitis Media: Untreated or inadequately treated AOM can progress to mastoiditis, meningitis, or chronic otitis media with hearing loss.
missedConsequences.Mastoiditis: Spread of infection to mastoid bone is a surgical emergency. Can lead to intracranial complications.
missedConsequences.Bacterial Meningitis: Young children with AOM can develop meningitis - look for lethargy, bulging fontanelle, neck stiffness.

commonPitfalls: Prescribing antibiotics for otitis media with effusion (OME) - OME is fluid without infection, doesn't need antibiotics
Missing the bulging TM - the key diagnostic criterion for AOM
Not recognizing that fever + ear tugging alone doesn't diagnose AOM - must visualize the TM
Under-dosing amoxicillin - high-dose (80-90 mg/kg/day) is standard for AOM
Not counseling on pain management - ibuprofen/acetaminophen should be offered regardless of antibiotic decision

allDiagnoses.name: Acute Otitis Media
allDiagnoses.rationale: Ear tugging, fever, URI symptoms, and irritability in a toddler - classic presentation pending otoscopic confirmation.

allDiagnoses.name: Otitis Media with Effusion
allDiagnoses.rationale: Middle ear fluid is common after URIs. Distinguished from AOM by LACK of acute inflammatory signs (no bulging, no erythema).

allDiagnoses.name: Mastoiditis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Complication of AOM - look for postauricular swelling, erythema, or protrusion of the ear.

allDiagnoses.name: Meningitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: High fever with lethargy in young child - always consider meningitis. AOM can be source.

allDiagnoses.name: Viral URI
allDiagnoses.rationale: May be concurrent, but ear tugging and high fever suggest more than simple URI.

allDiagnoses.name: Teething
allDiagnoses.rationale: Parents often attribute ear pulling to teething, but teething doesn't cause high fever.

toddler-18mo-aom.oldcartsNarrative: [Mother reporting]: "Lily has been pulling at her right ear since yesterday and crying a lot. She had a runny nose and cough for a few days before this started. Last night she barely slept - just kept crying and wouldn't let me put her down. She felt really hot this morning so I took her temperature and it was 102.4. She's been fussier than I've ever seen her. She's not eating well but she's drinking some. She goes to daycare three days a week. Her older brother had an ear infection last month."

historyQuestions.text: Which ear is she pulling at?
historyQuestions.answer: Mostly the right ear, but sometimes both. She really grabs at the right one and cries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Lateralizes symptoms but remember - toddlers can't localize pain well. Must examine both ears.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about the cold symptoms before the ear pain started.
historyQuestions.answer: She's had a runny nose for about 5 days - clear at first, then yellowish. Coughing, especially at night. A little fussy but not too bad until yesterday.
historyQuestions.reasoning: URI preceding ear pain is classic. Eustachian tube dysfunction from URI leads to middle ear fluid accumulation and secondary bacterial infection.

historyQuestions.text: When did the fever start and how high?
historyQuestions.answer: She felt warm yesterday afternoon but I didn't check. This morning it was 102.4. I gave her Tylenol and it came down some.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Acute onset of fever often marks transition from URI to AOM (bacterial superinfection).

historyQuestions.text: How was her sleep last night?
historyQuestions.answer: Terrible. She woke up screaming three times and would only calm down if I held her upright. Lying flat seemed to make it worse.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Lying flat increases pressure in middle ear, worsening pain. Classic AOM finding.

historyQuestions.text: How is she eating and drinking?
historyQuestions.answer: She doesn't want her bottle as much - only took half this morning. She'll drink from a sippy cup though. Not interested in food.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Decreased oral intake common with fever/illness but need to assess hydration status.

historyQuestions.text: How many wet diapers has she had today?
historyQuestions.answer: She's had 4 wet diapers since this morning, about normal for her.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Adequate urine output suggests adequate hydration despite decreased intake.

historyQuestions.text: Has she had ear infections before?
historyQuestions.answer: She had one about 4 months ago. It went away with the pink medicine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: History of AOM suggests recurrence risk. Amoxicillin worked before, suggests non-resistant organism.

historyQuestions.text: You mentioned daycare - how often does she go?
historyQuestions.answer: Three days a week. There's about 8 kids in her room.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Daycare is major risk factor for recurrent AOM due to viral exposure.

historyQuestions.text: Is she acting normally between the crying episodes? Any unusual sleepiness?
historyQuestions.answer: When the Tylenol kicks in she's more like herself - playing a little. Not super sleepy or out of it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CRITICAL question. Lethargy that doesn't improve with fever reduction is red flag for meningitis.

historyQuestions.text: Any vomiting or diarrhea?
historyQuestions.answer: She spit up once this morning but no real vomiting. No diarrhea.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Vomiting with fever and lethargy would raise concern for meningitis.

historyQuestions.text: Is she up to date on her vaccines?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, she's had all her shots.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Pneumococcal conjugate vaccine (PCV13) has reduced AOM incidence but not eliminated it.

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you currently taking?
historyQuestions.answer: Just blood pressure medicine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medications can cause symptoms and affect treatment choices.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No known drug allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical for safe prescribing.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any other medical conditions?
historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure. Otherwise healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Past medical history affects diagnosis and treatment.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

physicalExam.finding: Fussy, crying, irritable toddler who calms somewhat when held by mother. Alert and interactive between crying episodes. No toxic appearance.
physicalExam.reasoning: Non-toxic appearance is reassuring. Toxic appearance (lethargy, mottling) would be concerning.

physicalExam.finding: HR 130, RR 28, Temp 102.4°F (39.1°C), SpO2 98%, Weight 11kg
physicalExam.reasoning: Tachycardia and tachypnea consistent with fever. Weight needed for medication dosing.

physicalExam.finding: Moist mucous membranes, tears present when crying, skin turgor normal, capillary refill <2 seconds.
physicalExam.reasoning: Well-hydrated despite decreased intake. No concerning signs.

physicalExam.finding: RIGHT TM: Bulging, erythematous, opacified. Loss of light reflex. Decreased mobility on pneumatic otoscopy. Purulent effusion visible behind TM. No perforation.
physicalExam.reasoning: DIAGNOSTIC for AOM: bulging TM is the key finding. Bulging + erythema + effusion = AOM.

physicalExam.finding: LEFT TM: Mildly retracted, dull appearance, small air-fluid level visible. No bulging, no erythema.
physicalExam.reasoning: Left ear shows effusion WITHOUT inflammation - this is OME, not AOM. Common finding with recent URI.

physicalExam.finding: No postauricular swelling, erythema, or tenderness bilaterally. Ears not protruding.
physicalExam.reasoning: Rules out mastoiditis - a serious complication requiring ENT consult and IV antibiotics.

physicalExam.finding: Mild pharyngeal erythema. No tonsillar exudates. Postnasal drip present.
physicalExam.reasoning: Concurrent viral URI as expected. No strep pharyngitis features.

physicalExam.finding: Supple. No meningismus. Small, mobile, anterior cervical lymph nodes bilaterally.
physicalExam.reasoning: No neck stiffness - rules out meningitis clinically. Lymphadenopathy consistent with URI.

physicalExam.finding: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, crackles, or retractions. Upper airway congestion sounds transmitted.
physicalExam.reasoning: No lower respiratory involvement.

labs.name: No labs routinely indicated
labs.reasoning: Uncomplicated AOM is a clinical diagnosis. Labs not needed in well-appearing child with typical presentation.

labs.name: CBC (only if toxic-appearing)
labs.result: Not indicated for this well-appearing child.
labs.reasoning: CBC only if concern for sepsis or toxic appearance. This child is non-toxic.

imaging.name: No imaging routinely indicated
imaging.reasoning: Imaging only if concern for complications (mastoiditis - get CT temporal bone, intracranial extension).

imaging.name: CT Temporal Bone (if mastoiditis suspected)
imaging.result: Not indicated - no postauricular swelling or tenderness.
imaging.reasoning: CT only if clinical concern for mastoiditis: postauricular swelling, erythema, tenderness, protruding ear.

procedures.name: Pneumatic otoscopy
procedures.result: Decreased TM mobility on right (bulging), normal mobility on left.
procedures.reasoning: Pneumatic otoscopy improves diagnostic accuracy. Decreased/absent mobility confirms middle ear effusion.

procedures.name: Cerumen removal if needed
procedures.reasoning: Remove cerumen if blocking TM view. Curette preferred over irrigation in young children.

treatments.name: Amoxicillin high-dose (FIRST-LINE)
treatments.result: Amoxicillin 80-90 mg/kg/day divided BID x 10 days. For 11kg child: 500mg BID. Use liquid formulation.
treatments.reasoning: FIRST-LINE antibiotic. High-dose covers S. pneumoniae including intermediate resistance. 10 days for age <2 years. Give with food to reduce GI upset.

treatments.name: Amoxicillin-clavulanate (if treatment failure)
treatments.result: Would use if no improvement in 48-72 hours or recent amoxicillin use (within 30 days).
treatments.reasoning: Use amox-clav if: treatment failure on amoxicillin, amoxicillin within past 30 days, or concurrent purulent conjunctivitis (suggests H. influenzae). Dose: 90 mg/kg/day amoxicillin component.

treatments.name: Cephalosporin alternatives
treatments.result: If PCN allergic (non-anaphylactic): cefdinir 14 mg/kg/day divided BID or cefuroxime 30 mg/kg/day divided BID.
treatments.reasoning: Second-line for non-severe PCN allergy. If severe/anaphylactic PCN allergy: azithromycin (less effective but safe).

treatments.name: Pain management (ESSENTIAL)
treatments.result: Ibuprofen 10 mg/kg q6h (110mg = 5.5mL of 100mg/5mL) alternating with acetaminophen 15 mg/kg q4h PRN. Schedule for first 24-48 hours.
treatments.reasoning: PAIN MANAGEMENT IS CRITICAL regardless of antibiotic decision. Ear pain is severe. Schedule dosing for first 24-48 hours, not just PRN.

treatments.name: AVOID decongestants and antihistamines
treatments.result: No OTC cold medications. Not effective and potentially harmful in young children.
treatments.reasoning: Decongestants/antihistamines are NOT effective for AOM and may cause harm in young children. FDA recommends avoiding in <4 years.

treatments.name: Warm compress for comfort
treatments.result: Can apply warm (not hot) cloth to affected ear for comfort.
treatments.reasoning: Non-pharmacologic comfort measure. Some parents find this helpful.

consults.name: ENT (for recurrent AOM)
consults.result: Not needed for this episode. Refer if: 3+ episodes in 6 months, 4+ in 12 months, chronic OME >3 months, or concern for hearing loss.
consults.reasoning: ENT referral for recurrent AOM (consider tubes), chronic OME affecting hearing/speech, or complications.

disposition.name: Discharge home with antibiotics
disposition.result: Discharge home with amoxicillin prescription, pain management instructions, and return precautions.
disposition.reasoning: Uncomplicated AOM is managed as outpatient. Child is well-appearing, well-hydrated, and has reliable caregivers.

disposition.name: Antibiotic adherence counseling
disposition.result: Complete FULL 10-day course even if feeling better. Give with food. Store in refrigerator if liquid. Can mix with food/drink if needed.
disposition.reasoning: Emphasize completing full course. Common issue is stopping when child feels better. Incomplete treatment promotes resistance.

disposition.name: Follow-up plan
disposition.result: Routine follow-up in 10-14 days for ear recheck (sooner if not improving). Call/return if: fever persists >48h on antibiotics, symptoms worsen, new symptoms develop.
disposition.reasoning: Follow-up to confirm resolution. OME may persist 4-6 weeks after AOM - that's expected. Document hearing if OME persists >3 months.

disposition.name: Return precautions
disposition.result: Return if: fever not improving after 48-72h on antibiotics, increased irritability/lethargy, vomiting unable to keep down antibiotics, rash (allergic reaction), swelling behind ear (mastoiditis), ear discharge.
disposition.reasoning: Clear return precautions essential. Mastoiditis is the complication to watch for (postauricular swelling, ear pushed forward).

disposition.name: Left ear OME counseling
disposition.result: Left ear has fluid (OME) but not infected - doesn't need antibiotics. Will likely resolve in 4-6 weeks. Recheck at follow-up.
disposition.reasoning: Explain difference between AOM and OME. OME is common post-URI and usually resolves spontaneously. Only treat if persists >3 months with hearing concerns.

disposition.name: Prevention counseling
disposition.result: Prevention: breastfeeding (if applicable), avoid tobacco smoke exposure, avoid supine bottle feeding, vaccines up to date (PCV13, flu vaccine). Daycare exposure is difficult to modify.
disposition.reasoning: Discuss modifiable risk factors. PCV13 has reduced AOM incidence. Flu vaccine helps prevent viral URIs that precede AOM.

actualDiagnosis.name: Acute Otitis Media - Right Ear
teachingPoints: DIAGNOSTIC CRITERIA FOR AOM (AAP 2013): (1) Moderate-severe TM bulging, OR (2) New otorrhea not from otitis externa, OR (3) Mild TM bulging PLUS recent ear pain/erythema
BULGING is the key finding - erythema alone can be from crying and is not diagnostic
Distinguish AOM from OME: AOM has acute inflammation (bulging, erythema, pain, fever); OME has effusion without acute inflammation
HIGH-DOSE amoxicillin (80-90 mg/kg/day) is first-line - covers S. pneumoniae including intermediate resistance
Treatment decision depends on: Age, severity, unilateral vs bilateral, and certainty of diagnosis
PAIN MANAGEMENT is essential - ibuprofen/acetaminophen regardless of antibiotic decision
Watch for complications: Mastoiditis (postauricular swelling), TM perforation, meningitis

toddler-18mo-aom.teachingPoint: This toddler has classic AOM: preceding URI, acute fever, ear pain (manifested as pulling/crying), and the KEY FINDING of a bulging, erythematous tympanic membrane. Per AAP guidelines, this patient should be treated with antibiotics because she meets criteria: age <2 years + unilateral AOM with moderate symptoms. First-line is high-dose amoxicillin (80-90 mg/kg/day divided BID) for 10 days in children <2 years. Don't forget pain management - parents should give ibuprofen or acetaminophen scheduled for the first 48 hours. The left ear shows otitis media with effusion (OME) which doesn't require antibiotics - it's residual fluid from the URI that will typically resolve in 4-6 weeks.
toddler-18mo-aom.clinicalPearl: The hardest part of diagnosing AOM in children is getting a good view of the TM. Tips: (1) Use the largest speculum that fits, (2) Straighten the ear canal by pulling the pinna back and up in older children, back and down in infants, (3) Remove cerumen if blocking view - a curette is better than irrigation in children, (4) BULGING is the key diagnostic finding - crying causes erythema, so red TM alone doesn't diagnose AOM. Remember: 'When in doubt, watch it out' - observation for 48-72 hours with pain management is acceptable for mild AOM in children >2 years.

patientBackground.recentEvents: Patient was in usual state of health until symptoms began. No major recent life changes.
patientBackground.recentIllnesses: No significant recent illnesses other than this presentation.
patientBackground.contextualFactors: Otherwise healthy and active. This illness is unexpected and concerning.
patientBackground.supportSystem: Parents are primary caregivers and decision-makers. Very concerned about child.
patientBackground.healthBeliefs: Parents want the best for their child. Open to medical recommendations.
patientBackground.previousERVisits: Uses healthcare appropriately. No excessive ER utilization.

cases.title: Cough and Fast Breathing in an Infant

infant-4mo-rsv.name: Baby James W.
infant-4mo-rsv.chiefComplaint: Coughing and breathing fast for 2 days
infant-4mo-rsv.variantDescription: RSV bronchiolitis with moderate respiratory distress

missedConsequences.Bronchiolitis with Respiratory Failure: Progressive respiratory distress can lead to respiratory failure requiring intubation. Highest risk in young infants <3 months, premature infants.
missedConsequences.Apnea: Young infants with bronchiolitis can have apneic episodes - may require monitoring.
missedConsequences.Dehydration: Increased work of breathing prevents adequate oral intake, leading to dehydration.

commonPitfalls: Ordering CXR routinely - not indicated unless concern for bacterial pneumonia or other diagnosis
Using bronchodilators - albuterol is NOT recommended for bronchiolitis (no evidence of benefit)
Prescribing antibiotics - bronchiolitis is viral, antibiotics don't help
Not recognizing severity - infants can deteriorate quickly
Missing apnea risk in young infants - <2 months at highest risk

allDiagnoses.name: Bronchiolitis (RSV)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Classic presentation: infant <12 months with URI symptoms progressing to lower respiratory symptoms, wheezing, and respiratory distress.

allDiagnoses.name: Viral Upper Respiratory Infection
allDiagnoses.rationale: Concurrent diagnosis - the URI that preceded the bronchiolitis.

allDiagnoses.name: Bacterial Pneumonia
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can coexist with or complicate bronchiolitis. Consider if high fever, focal findings, or lack of improvement.

allDiagnoses.name: Pertussis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Consider in young infants with paroxysmal cough, post-tussive emesis, or incomplete immunization.

allDiagnoses.name: Foreign Body Aspiration
allDiagnoses.rationale: Consider if sudden onset, unilateral wheezing, or atypical presentation.

allDiagnoses.name: Reactive Airway Disease
allDiagnoses.rationale: True asthma is rare in infants <12 months. This presentation is more consistent with bronchiolitis.

allDiagnoses.name: Congenital Heart Disease
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would typically present earlier. But cardiac disease can present like respiratory illness.

infant-4mo-rsv.oldcartsNarrative: [Mother reporting]: "James started with a runny nose about 4 days ago. We thought it was just a cold. Then 2 days ago he started coughing a lot and breathing really fast. It's gotten worse - now I can see his ribs sticking out when he breathes. He's having trouble eating - he takes a few sucks of his bottle then stops to catch his breath. He's not as interested in eating as usual. He had a low fever yesterday. His 3-year-old sister had a bad cold last week. He was born full-term, no problems. He's had his 2-month shots."

historyQuestions.text: Tell me exactly how his feeding has been.
historyQuestions.answer: Usually he takes 5-6 ounces every 3-4 hours. Now he's only taking 2-3 ounces and it takes forever because he keeps stopping to breathe. Last bottle was 2 hours ago, he only took 2 ounces.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CRITICAL assessment. Decreased feeding due to respiratory distress indicates moderate-severe illness. Infants breathe through their nose - congestion + fast breathing = can't feed.

historyQuestions.text: How many wet diapers has he had in the last 24 hours?
historyQuestions.answer: Hmm, maybe 4? He usually has at least 6 or 7.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Decreased urine output suggests dehydration from decreased intake. Important for admission decision.

historyQuestions.text: Has the breathing gotten better or worse over the past day?
historyQuestions.answer: Definitely worse. Yesterday he was coughing a lot but breathing okay. Today I can see his ribs with each breath.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Bronchiolitis typically peaks day 3-5. Worsening respiratory distress raises concern.

historyQuestions.text: Have you noticed any pauses in his breathing or times when he turned blue?
historyQuestions.answer: No, nothing like that. He's breathing fast but hasn't stopped.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CRITICAL question in infants <2-3 months. Apnea with bronchiolitis is indication for admission.

historyQuestions.text: Was he born on time? Any problems after birth?
historyQuestions.answer: He was born at 39 weeks, healthy, came right home from the hospital.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prematurity significantly increases bronchiolitis severity. Full-term is reassuring.

historyQuestions.text: Anyone else at home sick?
historyQuestions.answer: His older sister had a really bad cold last week - runny nose, coughing. She's better now.
historyQuestions.reasoning: RSV spreads easily in households. Older sibling is classic source.

historyQuestions.text: Is he up to date on his vaccines?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, he's had his 2-month shots.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Only one dose of DTaP at 2 months - not fully protected against pertussis yet.

historyQuestions.text: Does he have any medical conditions? Any heart or lung problems?
historyQuestions.answer: No, he's been completely healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Underlying cardiac or pulmonary disease increases bronchiolitis severity.

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you currently taking?
historyQuestions.answer: Just blood pressure medicine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medications can cause symptoms and affect treatment choices.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No known drug allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical for safe prescribing.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

physicalExam.finding: Alert infant with visible respiratory distress. Not cyanotic. Tachypneic with accessory muscle use. Fussy but consolable.
physicalExam.reasoning: Alert is good. Visible distress but not cyanotic or lethargic - moderate severity.

physicalExam.finding: HR 160, RR 56, Temp 100.6°F (38.1°C), SpO2 92% on room air, Weight 6.5kg
physicalExam.reasoning: SpO2 92% is concerning - below target of 90-92% threshold. Tachypnea (normal RR for 4mo is 30-40).

physicalExam.finding: Subcostal retractions present. Intercostal retractions. Nasal flaring. Head bobbing with breathing. Expiratory grunting.
physicalExam.reasoning: Multiple signs of increased work of breathing. Grunting is concerning - creates auto-PEEP to maintain lung recruitment.

physicalExam.finding: Diffuse expiratory wheezes bilaterally. Coarse crackles throughout. Prolonged expiratory phase. Air movement present but decreased at bases.
physicalExam.reasoning: Classic bronchiolitis findings: wheezes + crackles from small airway inflammation and mucus plugging.

physicalExam.finding: Mucous membranes slightly dry. Anterior fontanelle flat. Skin turgor slightly decreased. Cap refill 2-3 seconds.
physicalExam.reasoning: Signs of mild dehydration - expected with decreased intake.

physicalExam.finding: Copious clear rhinorrhea. Congested-sounding.
physicalExam.reasoning: URI symptoms as expected.

physicalExam.finding: TMs slightly dull but not bulging, no erythema bilaterally.
physicalExam.reasoning: No concurrent AOM - common complication to look for.

labs.name: RSV rapid antigen or PCR
labs.reasoning: Confirms diagnosis for cohorting/isolation purposes. Doesn't change management but helpful for infection control and family counseling.

labs.name: Respiratory viral panel
labs.reasoning: Broader panel if atypical presentation or need to identify other viruses. Multiple viruses can cause bronchiolitis.

labs.name: CBC
labs.reasoning: Won't change management in typical bronchiolitis. Only if concern for bacterial superinfection or sepsis.

labs.name: Blood gas (VBG/ABG)
labs.result: Not indicated unless severe respiratory failure
labs.reasoning: Reserve for severe respiratory distress, impending respiratory failure, or when intubation being considered.

labs.name: BMP
labs.result: Not routinely indicated. Check if significant dehydration or IV fluids needed.
labs.reasoning: May be useful if starting IV fluids to guide management.

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.result: NOT routinely recommended per AAP guidelines.
imaging.reasoning: AAP guidelines recommend AGAINST routine CXR in bronchiolitis. CXR often shows non-specific findings (hyperinflation, atelectasis) that lead to unnecessary antibiotic use. Only if diagnostic uncertainty or concern for other diagnosis (pneumonia, foreign body).

procedures.name: Nasal suctioning
procedures.result: Gentle nasal suctioning performed with bulb suction. Copious clear secretions removed. Infant more comfortable.
procedures.reasoning: Nasal suctioning improves comfort and may help feeding. Infants are obligate nasal breathers. Use bulb suction or gentle wall suction. Avoid deep/aggressive suctioning.

procedures.name: Supplemental oxygen
procedures.result: Supplemental O2 via nasal cannula started at 1 L/min. SpO2 improved to 95%.
procedures.reasoning: Supplemental O2 to maintain SpO2 ≥90% (or ≥92% in some guidelines). NC is first-line. High-flow nasal cannula (HFNC) if escalation needed.

procedures.name: IV access
procedures.result: Peripheral IV placed for IV fluid administration.
procedures.reasoning: IV access for hydration since oral intake decreased. Maintenance fluids + deficit replacement.

treatments.name: Supplemental oxygen (CORE TREATMENT)
treatments.result: O2 via nasal cannula to maintain SpO2 ≥90-92%. Start at 0.5-1 L/min, titrate as needed.
treatments.reasoning: Supplemental oxygen is cornerstone of treatment. Target SpO2 ≥90% (AAP) or ≥92% (some institutions). Wean as tolerated.

treatments.name: IV fluids for hydration
treatments.result: D5 1/2NS at maintenance rate (26 mL/hr for 6.5kg) with bolus for dehydration. NPO during respiratory distress.
treatments.reasoning: Ensure adequate hydration. Infants with respiratory distress have difficulty feeding safely. IV fluids until respiratory status improves enough for oral feeding trial.

treatments.name: NO bronchodilators (albuterol)
treatments.result: Bronchodilators NOT given. AAP recommends against routine use in bronchiolitis.
treatments.reasoning: CRITICAL: Albuterol does NOT help bronchiolitis. Multiple RCTs show no benefit. The wheezing is from airway inflammation and mucus, not bronchospasm. Do NOT give albuterol.

treatments.name: NO corticosteroids
treatments.result: Steroids NOT given. No evidence of benefit in bronchiolitis.
treatments.reasoning: Steroids do NOT help bronchiolitis. Unlike asthma, steroids have no benefit in RSV bronchiolitis. Do NOT give dexamethasone/prednisone.

treatments.name: NO antibiotics
treatments.result: Antibiotics NOT given. Bronchiolitis is viral.
treatments.reasoning: Bronchiolitis is VIRAL - antibiotics don't help. Only give antibiotics if documented bacterial superinfection (rare).

treatments.name: Supportive care (suctioning, positioning)
treatments.result: Nasal suctioning PRN before feeds. Elevate head of bed. Minimize handling to reduce oxygen demand.
treatments.reasoning: Nasal suctioning before feeds helps intake. Avoid excessive suctioning. Cluster care to minimize disturbance.

treatments.name: High-flow nasal cannula (if escalation needed)
treatments.result: HFNC available if conventional NC insufficient. Not needed currently.
treatments.reasoning: HFNC provides heated humidified oxygen and mild CPAP effect. Use if conventional NC insufficient to maintain oxygenation or work of breathing severe.

treatments.name: Hypertonic saline (3%) - optional
treatments.result: May consider hypertonic saline nebulization in admitted patients.
treatments.reasoning: Some evidence hypertonic saline may reduce hospital stay in admitted patients. AAP says may be considered. Variable practice.

consults.name: PICU (if severe/deteriorating)
consults.result: Not needed currently. Would consult for: respiratory failure, need for HFNC/CPAP, significant apnea, impending intubation.
consults.reasoning: PICU involvement for severe bronchiolitis requiring respiratory support beyond conventional NC.

disposition.name: Admit to pediatrics
disposition.result: Admitted to pediatric unit for supplemental oxygen, IV hydration, and close respiratory monitoring.
disposition.reasoning: Admission criteria met: SpO2 <92%, moderate respiratory distress, decreased oral intake. Monitor for deterioration - bronchiolitis peaks day 3-5.

disposition.name: Admission criteria review
disposition.result: Meets admission criteria: SpO2 92% (below threshold), retractions with grunting, decreased feeding with mild dehydration.
disposition.reasoning: Admission criteria: SpO2 <90-92%, significant respiratory distress, inadequate oral intake, apnea, age <2-3 months, high-risk conditions.

disposition.name: Continuous monitoring
disposition.result: Continuous pulse oximetry. Respiratory rate and work of breathing assessment q2-4h. Strict I/O.
disposition.reasoning: Close monitoring essential. Watch for deterioration, apnea (especially young infants), and dehydration.

disposition.name: Apnea monitoring
disposition.result: Apnea monitoring in place. Parents counseled about apnea risk in young infants.
disposition.reasoning: Young infants (<2-3 months) at risk for apnea with RSV. This 4-month-old is just above highest risk age but still warrants vigilance.

disposition.name: Feeding plan
disposition.result: NPO initially with IV fluids. Trial oral feeds when RR <60 and work of breathing improved. Small frequent feeds.
disposition.reasoning: Infants with respiratory distress cannot safely coordinate suck-swallow-breathe. NPO with IV fluids until improved, then careful feeding trials.

disposition.name: Discharge criteria
disposition.result: Discharge when: SpO2 ≥90% on room air for ≥12h, adequate oral intake (>50% of normal), no significant respiratory distress, parents comfortable with home care.
disposition.reasoning: Clear discharge criteria. Respiratory support and feeding adequacy are key. Home suction devices may help.

disposition.name: Parent education
disposition.result: Counseled: Disease peaks day 3-5, expect several more days of symptoms at home. Nasal suctioning, small frequent feeds, watch for danger signs.
disposition.reasoning: Set expectations - bronchiolitis lasts 1-2 weeks. Teach suctioning. Return precautions for worsening.

disposition.name: Return precautions (for discharge)
disposition.result: Return if: breathing pauses, turns blue, severe retractions, unable to feed, fewer wet diapers, more sleepy than usual.
disposition.reasoning: Clear return precautions for parents. Focus on breathing, feeding, and mental status.

disposition.name: RSV isolation precautions
disposition.result: Contact isolation for RSV. Strict hand hygiene. Cohort with other RSV patients if possible.
disposition.reasoning: RSV is highly contagious. Contact precautions, hand hygiene, and cohorting prevent nosocomial spread.

actualDiagnosis.name: RSV Bronchiolitis - Moderate Severity
teachingPoints: BRONCHIOLITIS IS A CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS: URI symptoms → lower respiratory symptoms in infant <12-24 months
SUPPORTIVE CARE ONLY: No bronchodilators (albuterol doesn't help), no steroids, no antibiotics
KEY INTERVENTIONS: Supplemental O2 to maintain SpO2 ≥90%, nasal suctioning, ensure hydration
ADMISSION CRITERIA: SpO2 <90-92%, significant respiratory distress, inability to maintain hydration, apnea, age <3 months, high-risk conditions
CXR NOT RECOMMENDED routinely - only if diagnostic uncertainty or concern for other diagnosis
DISEASE COURSE: Typically worsens days 3-5, then improves. Full recovery 2-3 weeks.
PREVENTION: RSV immunoprophylaxis (palivizumab) for high-risk infants; hand hygiene is key
NEW: RSV vaccines now available for pregnant women and older adults

infant-4mo-rsv.teachingPoint: This infant has moderate bronchiolitis and meets criteria for admission: SpO2 92% (below threshold), significant work of breathing with retractions and grunting, decreased feeding, and mild dehydration. Management is SUPPORTIVE: (1) Supplemental oxygen to maintain SpO2 ≥90%, (2) Nasal suctioning to clear secretions, (3) IV fluids if unable to maintain oral hydration. What NOT to do: No albuterol (doesn't help in bronchiolitis), no steroids (no evidence of benefit), no antibiotics (viral illness), no routine CXR (doesn't change management). This is a common admission that tests your ability to NOT over-treat.
infant-4mo-rsv.clinicalPearl: The AAP bronchiolitis guidelines emphasize what NOT to do because overtreatment is common: no routine CXR, no bronchodilators, no steroids, no antibiotics. The most important treatments are the simplest: oxygen, suctioning, and hydration. Remember that bronchiolitis peaks around day 3-5 of illness, so an infant getting worse on day 2-3 is following the expected course. The exception: young infants <2-3 months are at risk for APNEA with bronchiolitis - this is an indication for admission regardless of other findings.

actualDiagnosis.name: Health Supervision - 2 Month Well Child Visit
teachingPoints: KEY 2-MONTH MILESTONES: Social smile (most important), cooing, lifts head when prone, follows past midline, recognizes parents
GROWTH: Expect weight gain of ~1 oz/day (30g/day). Birth weight should double by 4-5 months.
IMMUNIZATIONS: DTaP, IPV, Hib, PCV13, Rotavirus, +/- HepB. Counsel on side effects: fussiness, fever, injection site reactions.
SAFE SLEEP: ABCs - Alone, on Back, in Crib. No soft bedding, bumpers, or toys. Room-sharing (not bed-sharing) recommended for first 6-12 months.
BREASTFEEDING: Continue exclusive breastfeeding for 6 months if possible. Vitamin D supplementation (400 IU/day) for breastfed infants.
RED FLAGS requiring evaluation: No social smile by 8 weeks, poor feeding, no weight gain, weak cry, floppy tone, absent red reflex
ANTICIPATORY GUIDANCE: Tummy time, reading to baby, responding to cues, recognizing hunger/fullness signals
MATERNAL WELLBEING: Screen for postpartum depression at every infant visit

healthy-2mo.teachingPoint: The 2-month well child visit focuses on: (1) GROWTH - this baby is tracking on the 50th percentile which is perfect, (2) DEVELOPMENT - social smile is THE key milestone at this age, and Emma has it, (3) FEEDING - breastfeeding is going well with appropriate output, (4) IMMUNIZATIONS - this is the first major vaccine visit, so addressing parental concerns is important, (5) SAFETY - safe sleep practices, car seat use, (6) MATERNAL WELLBEING - always screen for postpartum depression. Key physical exam findings: hip stability (Barlow/Ortolani), red reflex, femoral pulses (for coarctation), and developmental assessment.
healthy-2mo.clinicalPearl: The well child visit follows a predictable structure: (1) Growth assessment and plotting, (2) Developmental surveillance, (3) Physical examination with age-appropriate maneuvers, (4) Immunizations, (5) Anticipatory guidance, (6) Address parental concerns. At 2 months, the SOCIAL SMILE is the most important milestone - if absent by 8 weeks, evaluate for developmental delay or hearing/vision problems. Remember to screen for POSTPARTUM DEPRESSION - it affects up to 15% of mothers and the pediatric visit is often the only medical contact in the postpartum period.

imaging.name: Chest X-ray (NOT routinely indicated)
imaging.result: CXR not needed for typical asthma exacerbation. Would show hyperinflation only.
imaging.reasoning: CXR does NOT change management in typical exacerbation. Only obtain if: (1) Fever/concern for pneumonia, (2) First-time wheeze/foreign body concern, (3) Asymmetric findings, (4) Not responding to treatment as expected, (5) Concern for pneumothorax.

procedures.name: Albuterol Nebulizer 2.5mg q20min x 3
procedures.result: Albuterol 2.5mg neb given x 3 doses over 1 hour. SpO2 improved 91% → 95% after second treatment. Wheezing improved, retractions decreased.
procedures.reasoning: FIRST-LINE treatment. Beta-agonist bronchodilator. Repeat every 20 minutes x 3 for acute exacerbation. Can give continuous (10-15mg/hr) for severe exacerbation.

procedures.name: Ipratropium Bromide 500mcg with first 3 albuterol treatments
procedures.result: Ipratropium 500mcg added to each of first 3 albuterol nebulizers.
procedures.reasoning: ADD IPRATROPIUM for moderate-severe exacerbations. Anticholinergic bronchodilator - synergistic with albuterol. Give with first 3 treatments, not maintenance.

procedures.name: Oral Corticosteroids - Prednisone 2mg/kg (max 60mg)
procedures.result: Prednisone 40mg PO given (2mg/kg for this 20kg child). Will continue 1-2mg/kg daily for 3-5 days.
procedures.reasoning: GIVE EARLY - systemic steroids reduce inflammation, speed recovery, prevent relapse. Takes 4-6 hours to work so don't delay! Oral is as effective as IV. Duration 3-5 days for exacerbation.

procedures.name: Supplemental Oxygen
procedures.result: 2L nasal cannula applied. SpO2 improved from 91% to 96%.
procedures.reasoning: Target SpO2 ≥94%. Hypoxemia from V/Q mismatch. Supplemental O2 as needed.

procedures.name: IV Access (if not responding)
procedures.result: NOT needed - patient responding to nebulizers.
procedures.reasoning: IV access for: magnesium, IV steroids (if vomiting), or if likely to deteriorate. Not needed for moderate exacerbation responding to treatment.

procedures.name: IV Magnesium Sulfate 50mg/kg (max 2g)
procedures.result: NOT indicated - patient responding to bronchodilators.
procedures.reasoning: Magnesium is smooth muscle relaxant. Use for SEVERE exacerbations not responding to initial bronchodilators. Give over 20 minutes. Watch for hypotension.

procedures.name: IM Epinephrine (if severe/not responding)
procedures.result: NOT indicated - patient responding to nebs.
procedures.reasoning: Epinephrine 0.01mg/kg IM (max 0.5mg) for severe exacerbations not responding to nebs. Also consider for anaphylaxis component.

treatments.name: Discharge Medications
treatments.result: Prednisone 20mg BID x 5 days. Albuterol MDI 2 puffs q4-6h PRN. RESTART Flovent 110mcg 2 puffs BID.
treatments.reasoning: Complete 3-5 day steroid burst. Rescue inhaler with spacer. CRITICAL: restart controller medication - non-adherence caused this exacerbation.

treatments.name: Spacer Device with MDI
treatments.result: Spacer provided. Technique demonstrated and return-demonstrated by mother.
treatments.reasoning: Spacer ESSENTIAL for MDI use in children - improves drug delivery. Teach proper technique. Return demonstration confirms understanding.

treatments.name: Written Asthma Action Plan
treatments.result: Written asthma action plan provided with green/yellow/red zones. Reviewed with family.
treatments.reasoning: EVERY asthmatic needs written action plan. Green (daily controller), Yellow (increase treatment when symptomatic), Red (emergency signs - call 911 or go to ED).

treatments.name: Trigger Avoidance Counseling
treatments.result: Discussed cat and dust triggers. Provided allergen reduction strategies. Consider allergy referral.
treatments.reasoning: Allergen exposure worsens asthma. Cat should ideally not be in child's bedroom. HEPA filters, dust mite covers may help.

treatments.name: Influenza Vaccine Recommendation
treatments.result: Flu vaccine recommended annually for all asthmatics.
treatments.reasoning: Viral infections trigger most pediatric asthma exacerbations. Flu vaccine reduces risk.

consults.name: PCP Follow-up in 1-2 weeks
consults.result: Follow-up with pediatrician in 1 week to assess controller medication adherence and response.
consults.reasoning: Close follow-up after exacerbation essential. Assess control, reinforce education, adjust medications if needed.

consults.name: Pediatric Pulmonology Referral
consults.result: Consider referral given hospitalization history and poor control.
consults.reasoning: Refer for: frequent exacerbations, prior hospitalization, ICU/intubation history, poor control despite adherence, diagnostic uncertainty.

consults.name: Allergy/Immunology Referral
consults.result: Consider allergy testing given known cat/dust triggers.
consults.reasoning: Allergy evaluation useful for: identifying triggers, considering immunotherapy, assessing allergic component.

consults.name: Social Work (if barriers to adherence)
consults.result: Discussed barriers to medication adherence. Ran out of Flovent due to cost. Social work consulted for medication assistance programs.
consults.reasoning: Address barriers to adherence: cost, understanding, access, health literacy. Medication assistance programs available.

disposition.name: Discharge Home (if good response)
disposition.result: Patient discharged after 2-hour observation. SpO2 sustained 96% on room air. No retractions. Speaking in sentences.
disposition.reasoning: Discharge criteria: SpO2 >94% sustained on RA, minimal/no respiratory distress, speaking in sentences, good response lasting >60 minutes after last neb, able to take PO, reliable family.

disposition.name: Admission Criteria
disposition.result: NOT indicated - patient responded well.
disposition.reasoning: Admit if: SpO2 <94% despite treatment, ongoing respiratory distress, poor response to 3 nebs, unable to take PO, unreliable follow-up, prior intubation.

disposition.name: Return Precautions
disposition.result: Return if: increased work of breathing, unable to speak in sentences, blue lips, not improving with rescue inhaler, drowsy or hard to wake.
disposition.reasoning: Family must know warning signs for return to ED. Written instructions provided.

actualDiagnosis.name: Moderate Acute Asthma Exacerbation
teachingPoints: SEVERITY ASSESSMENT: Mild (SpO2 >94%, sentences), Moderate (SpO2 90-94%, phrases), Severe (SpO2 <90%, words), Life-threatening (silent chest)
INITIAL TREATMENT: Albuterol nebs q20min x3 (or continuous), systemic steroids, supplemental O2
ADD IPRATROPIUM: For moderate-severe exacerbations - gives additional bronchodilation
STEROIDS: Give early. Oral is as effective as IV. Prednisone/prednisolone 1-2 mg/kg (max 60mg)
REASSESS AFTER TREATMENT: Look for improvement in SpO2, work of breathing, peak flow
DISPOSITION: Home if good response, sustained SpO2 >94%, no retractions. Admit if poor response.
CONTROLLER MEDICATION: This patient was non-adherent - critical to restart and ensure understanding
ASTHMA ACTION PLAN: Provide written plan with green/yellow/red zones

moderate-exacerbation.teachingPoint: This child has a moderate asthma exacerbation triggered by viral URI, worsened by non-adherence to controller medication. Treatment: (1) Oxygen to keep SpO2 >94%, (2) Albuterol nebs q20min x3 with ipratropium, (3) Systemic steroids immediately. After treatment: SpO2 improved to 95% - can likely discharge if sustained. Critical: restart Flovent and provide asthma action plan. Address barriers to medication adherence (cost, understanding).
moderate-exacerbation.clinicalPearl: The most important predictor of future severe exacerbation is past severe exacerbation. Other risk factors: ICU admission, intubation, recent ED visit, poor adherence to controllers, heavy rescue inhaler use (>1 canister/month). Always ask about these in the history. Remember: steroids take 4-6 hours to work, so give them EARLY.
